template_description: Social-ecological system template for marine fisheries, covering fishing activities, stock depletion pressures, ecosystem service impacts, and fisheries management responses. Applicable to temperate offshore systems.

connections.description: Market demand for cod directly drives trawling effort in the Baltic. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Excessive trawling effort has driven Eastern Baltic cod below safe biological limits
Bottom trawls physically disturb soft-sediment seabed habitats. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features.

Gillnets and trawls incidentally catch harbour porpoise and diving seabirds. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod affects Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass
Hypoxic conditions eliminate benthic fauna over large areas of the Baltic deep basins
Hypoxia reduces cod spawning success and juvenile survival in deep-water spawning areas
Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass affects Commercial fish stock provision (cod, herring, sprat)
Commercial fish stock provision (cod, herring, sprat) affects Fishing fleet economic viability
Baltic cod fishing quota reductions (EU TAC) affects Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
Spatial closures prevent trawl damage in ecologically sensitive spawning areas. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features.
Government renewable energy targets drive rapid expansion of offshore wind installations
Pile-driving during construction generates intense underwater noise affecting marine mammals

Market demand for herring drives pelagic fishing fleet effort in the Baltic Sea. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Bottom trawling resuspends organic sediments and increases oxygen demand, exacerbating deep-water hypoxia in enclosed basins
Deep-water renewal events bring oxygenated water below the halocline, directly controlling benthic macrofauna survival in Baltic basins

The primary causal direction is hypoxia affecting cod, not overfishing causing hypoxia. While trophic cascade effects are possible, the link from cod stock reduction to deep-water oxygen conditions is very indirect. ICES assessments emphasize hypoxia as driver of cod decline. Strength and confidence reduced.
Cod collapse released sprat from predation pressure, causing sprat to outcompete herring larvae for zooplankton prey, depressing herring recruitment through competitive exclusion

Cod quota reductions redirect fishing effort toward pelagic species (herring, sprat), increasing pressure on small pelagic stocks as fishers substitute target species. Note: positive polarity reflects fleet displacement effect - when cod quotas are cut, fishing effort shifts to pelagic species (herring and sprat)

Contamination from dumped munitions (TNT, heavy metals) would harm cod through toxicological effects, not increase biomass. Polarity corrected from + to -. Effect size is uncertain; strength to weak, confidence to 2.

Military munition dumping introduces toxic compounds harmful to marine life including cod. Polarity corrected from + to -. Limited evidence for population-level effects. Strength to weak, confidence to 2.

Harbour porpoise bycatch mitigation measures (pingers, gear modifications) do not directly address cod overfishing. While some gear changes may indirectly affect fishing effort, the primary target is bycatch reduction. Strength and confidence reduced.

Offshore wind farm development creates trawling exclusion zones, reducing demersal trawling activity in those areas. Multiple North Sea and Baltic studies document fisheries displacement from wind farm areas. Polarity corrected from + to -.
Seasonal closures are the primary implementation tool for Baltic cod quota reductions
Acoustic pingers implement harbour porpoise bycatch mitigation requirements (EU Reg 812/2004)
Fishing fleet economic viability reinforces Demand for Baltic cod and herring
Energy security from Baltic wind farms reinforces Offshore wind energy expansion targets
Fish processing industry employment reinforces demand for Baltic cod and herring fisheries
Maritime trade revenue reinforces demand for shipping routes through the Baltic
